title: Fair Division (Part I) -- Divisible Goods and Cake Cutting

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe fair division and its role in multi-agent systems? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Fair division studies how to divide one or more goods among agents according to suitable fairness criteria
Fair division is a sub-area of multi-agent resource allocation distinguished by its explicit focus on fairness
Fair division is irrelevant to MAS because collaborative agents never need to agree on resource allocations
Fairness guarantees matter in MAS applications because they help maintain trust and participation
Fair division only matters in economics and has no connection to electronic commerce or collaborative problem solving

explanations.text: This is the core definition given in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Fair division is the problem of dividing one or several goods amongst two or more agents in a way that satisfies a suitable fairness criterion

explanations.text: The notes explicitly place fair division inside MARA while distinguishing it by its fairness focus.
explanations.notes_quote: It is a sub-area of the broader field of multi-agent resource allocation (MARA), distinguished by its explicit focus on fairness concerns

explanations.text: The notes say the opposite: collaborative problem solving often requires agents to agree on a suitable division of resources first.
explanations.notes_quote: In collaborative problem solving, agents must first agree on a suitable division of available resources

explanations.text: This is one of the explicit reasons fair division is central in MAS.
explanations.notes_quote: Users and platform developers need fairness guarantees to maintain trust and participation

explanations.text: The notes explicitly connect fair division to MAS, collaborative problem solving, and electronic commerce.
explanations.notes_quote: Typical MAS application areas (e.g., electronic commerce) are closely tied to resource allocation

questions.source_ref: Section 1.2: Setting; Section 1.3: Types of Goods
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the setting and type restrictions used this week? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The agent set is a finite set $N = \{1, \ldots, n\}$
This week focuses on divisible goods such as cake cutting
The notes restrict attention to goods that are static, available in single units, and non-sharable
This week focuses primarily on indivisible goods, while divisible goods are deferred to next week
Goods can be classified by divisibility, temporal nature, sharability, and multiplicity

explanations.text: This is the formal agent set used in the setting.
explanations.notes_quote: A finite set of agents $N = \{1, \ldots, n\}$

explanations.text: The week is explicitly about divisible goods, specifically cake cutting.
explanations.notes_quote: This week focuses on divisible goods (cake cutting)

explanations.text: These are the simplifying restrictions stated in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: We restrict our attention to goods that are static, available in single units, and non-sharable

explanations.text: The notes say the reverse: divisible goods are covered this week, and indivisible goods next week.
explanations.notes_quote: This week focuses on divisible goods (cake cutting). Next week covers indivisible goods

explanations.text: These four dimensions are listed in the classification table.
explanations.notes_quote: Divisibility ... Temporal nature ... Sharability ... Multiplicity

questions.source_ref: Section 2.1: The Model; Section 2.2: Valuation Functions
questions.text: Which of the following are part of the standard cake-cutting model used in the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The cake is modelled as the unit interval $[0,1]$
Each piece $X_i$ may be a finite union of disjoint subintervals
Valuation functions must be additive and normalized so that $v_i([0,1]) = 1$
Valuation functions are allowed to assign negative value to disliked cake regions
Divisibility means that for any interval $I$ and $\lambda \in [0,1]$, there exists a subinterval worth exactly $\lambda \cdot v_i(I)$ to agent $i$

explanations.text: This is the basic geometric model used throughout the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: We model the cake as the unit interval $[0, 1]$

explanations.text: The notes allow pieces to be finite unions of disjoint subintervals.
explanations.notes_quote: Pieces of cake $X_1, \ldots, X_m \subseteq [0, 1]$, each a finite union of disjoint subintervals

explanations.text: Additivity and normalization are two of the three required valuation properties.
explanations.notes_quote: Additivity ... Normalization: $v_i([0, 1]) = 1$

explanations.text: The notes require non-negative valuation functions, not negative values for disliked regions.
explanations.notes_quote: Each player $i \in N$ has a non-negative, absolutely continuous valuation function

explanations.text: This is exactly the divisibility property stated in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Given an interval $I$ and $\lambda \in [0, 1]$, there exists a subinterval $I' \subseteq I$ such that $v_i(I') = \lambda \cdot v_i(I)$

questions.text: In the worked example where Agent 1 only values chocolate on $[0,1/4]$ and Agent 2 values the whole cake uniformly, which of the following statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: For allocation $A_1 = [0,1/2], A_2 = [1/2,1]$, the utilities are $v_1(A_1)=1$ and $v_2(A_2)=1/2$
For allocation $A_1 = [0,1/4], A_2 = [1/4,1]$, the utilities are $v_1(A_1)=1$ and $v_2(A_2)=3/4$
For allocation $A_1 = [0,1/5], A_2 = [1/5,1]$, both agents get utility $4/5$
An allocation is complete only if every agent receives a connected interval
Completeness means $\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_i = [0,1]$

explanations.text: These are the exact values listed in the worked example table.
This is the second allocation in the worked example.
This is the equitable example from the worked example table.
Completeness concerns covering the whole cake, not whether each piece is connected.
explanations.notes_quote: An allocation is complete if $\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_i = [0, 1]$

explanations.text: This is the definition of a complete allocation.
explanations.notes_quote: An allocation is complete if $\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_i = [0, 1]$

questions.source_ref: Section 3.1: Proportionality; Section 3.2: Envy-Freeness; Section 3.3: Equitability; Section 3.4: Pareto-Optimality
questions.text: Which of the following statements correctly match the four main solution concepts in cake cutting? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Proportionality requires each agent to value their own share at least $1/n$
Envy-freeness requires that no agent values another agent's piece more than their own
Equitability requires all agents to receive pieces of equal geometric length
Pareto-optimality means there is no other allocation that makes someone strictly better off without making anyone worse off
Equitability requires all agents to assign the same value to their own allocated pieces

explanations.text: This is the formal definition of proportionality.
explanations.notes_quote: $v_i(A_i) \geq \frac{1}{n} \quad \text{for all } i \in N$

explanations.text: This is exactly the envy-freeness condition.
explanations.notes_quote: $v_i(A_i) \geq v_i(A_j) \quad \text{for all } i, j \in N$

explanations.text: Equitability is about equal subjective value, not equal physical size or length.
explanations.notes_quote: An allocation is equitable if all agents have the same value for their allocated piece

explanations.text: This is the standard Pareto-optimality definition used in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: there is no other allocation ... such that $v_i(B_i) \geq v_i(A_i)$ for all $i$ ... and $v_i(B_i) > v_i(A_i)$ for some $i$

explanations.text: Equitability compares the values agents assign to their own shares.
explanations.notes_quote: $v_i(A_i) = v_j(A_j) \quad \text{for all } i, j \in N$

questions.text: Using the worked example summary table, which of the following classifications are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $A_1=[0,1/2], A_2=[1/2,1]$ is proportional and envy-free, but not equitable and not Pareto-optimal
$A_1=[0,1/4], A_2=[1/4,1]$ is proportional, envy-free, and Pareto-optimal, but not equitable
$A_1=[0,1/5], A_2=[1/5,1]$ is equitable and proportional
$A_1=[0,1/2], A_2=[1/2,1]$ is not envy-free because Agent 2 values Agent 1's piece more
$A_1=[0,1/4], A_2=[1/4,1]$ is not proportional because Agent 2 gets less than $1/2$

explanations.text: This matches the summary table exactly.
This is the second row of the summary table.
The notes explicitly identify this allocation as equitable, and it also satisfies proportionality.
Agent 2 values both halves equally at $1/2$, so there is no envy in that allocation.
explanations.notes_quote: Agent 2 has $v_2(A_1) = 1/2$, $v_2(A_2) = 1/2$. No envy -- Envy-free

explanations.text: Agent 2 gets $3/4$, which is above the proportional threshold of $1/2$ for two players.

questions.source_ref: Section 4.1: Two Players: Proportionality iff Envy-Freeness
questions.text: For two players, which of the following statements are correct about proportionality and envy-freeness? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: For two players, envy-freeness implies proportionality
For two players, proportionality implies envy-freeness
For two players, proportionality is strictly weaker than envy-freeness
The proof uses the fact that for player 1, $v_1(A_1)+v_1(A_2)=1$
For two players, an allocation can be envy-free without being proportional

explanations.text: This is one direction of the equivalence theorem.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.1: Two Players: Proportionality iff Envy-Freeness
explanations.notes_quote: For two players, an allocation is proportional if and only if it is envy-free

explanations.text: This is the converse direction, also proved in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.1: Two Players: Proportionality iff Envy-Freeness
explanations.notes_quote: For two players, an allocation is proportional if and only if it is envy-free

explanations.text: For two players they are equivalent, not strictly ordered.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.1: Two Players: Proportionality iff Envy-Freeness
explanations.notes_quote: For two players, an allocation is proportional if and only if it is envy-free

explanations.text: The proof relies on normalization over the two allocated pieces summing to 1.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.1: Two Players: Proportionality iff Envy-Freeness

explanations.text: That cannot happen for two players because the two notions coincide.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.1: Two Players: Proportionality iff Envy-Freeness

questions.source_ref: Section 4.2: Three or More Players: Envy-Freeness implies Proportionality (but NOT vice versa)
questions.text: For three or more players, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Envy-freeness implies proportionality
Proportionality implies envy-freeness
There are proportional allocations that are not envy-free
The notes give a counterexample where player 1 gets value exactly $1/3$ for their own piece but values player 2's piece at $1/3+\varepsilon$
For $n \geq 3$, proportionality and envy-freeness are equivalent just as in the two-player case

explanations.text: This is the theorem stated in the notes for $n \geq 3$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.2: Three or More Players: Envy-Freeness implies Proportionality (but NOT vice versa)
explanations.notes_quote: For three or more players, envy-freeness implies proportionality, but the converse is not true

explanations.text: The converse fails for three or more players.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.2: Three or More Players: Envy-Freeness implies Proportionality (but NOT vice versa)
explanations.notes_quote: the converse is not true

explanations.text: This is exactly what the counterexample establishes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.2: Three or More Players: Envy-Freeness implies Proportionality (but NOT vice versa)
explanations.notes_quote: Counterexample (PROP $\not\Rightarrow$ EF)

explanations.text: That is the specific numerical structure of the counterexample in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.2: Three or More Players: Envy-Freeness implies Proportionality (but NOT vice versa)
explanations.notes_quote: Let $v_1(A_1) = 1/3$, $v_1(A_2) = 1/3 + \varepsilon$, $v_1(A_3) = 1/3 - \varepsilon$

explanations.text: Equivalence holds only for two players, not for three or more.
explanations.notes_quote: For n >= 3: Envy-free => Proportional (but NOT the reverse)

sections.title: 5. Computation and the Robertson-Webb Query Model

questions.source_ref: Section 5: Computation: Can We Find Fair Allocations Efficiently?; Section 6: The Robertson-Webb Query Model
questions.text: Which of the following statements about computation and the Robertson-Webb model are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The Robertson-Webb model measures complexity by counting Eval and Cut queries
$\mathrm{Eval}_i(x,y)$ asks how much player $i$ values the interval $[x,y]$
$\mathrm{Cut}_i(x,\alpha)$ returns a point $y$ such that player $i$ values $[x,y]$ at exactly $\alpha$
The notes claim complete envy-free allocations for general $n$ are impossible to compute
The notes state that complete envy-free allocations exist, but the best known general algorithm has astronomically high runtime

explanations.text: This is the explicit complexity measure used in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: The complexity of a cake-cutting algorithm is measured by the total number of Eval and Cut queries it uses

explanations.text: This is the definition of an evaluation query.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{Eval}_i(x, y) \quad \text{returns } v_i([x, y])$

explanations.text: This is the definition of a cut query.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{Cut}_i(x, \alpha) \quad \text{returns } y \text{ such that } v_i([x, y]) = \alpha$

explanations.text: The notes say such allocations do exist; the issue is computational cost, not impossibility.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5: Computation: Can We Find Fair Allocations Efficiently?
explanations.notes_quote: Envy-freeness AND completeness: Yes, such allocations exist

explanations.text: This is the key computational message of the section.
explanations.section_ref: Section 5: Computation: Can We Find Fair Allocations Efficiently?
explanations.notes_quote: the best known algorithm for $n$ agents has runtime $O(n^{n^{n^{n^{n^n}}}})$ -- astronomically high

questions.text: Which of the following statements about Cut-and-Choose are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Player 1 cuts the cake into two pieces they value equally, and Player 2 chooses first
Cut-and-Choose is envy-free for two players
Because it is envy-free for two players, it is also proportional
Cut-and-Choose is always equitable
Its Robertson-Webb complexity is 2 operations: 1 Cut query and 1 Eval query

explanations.text: That is the exact two-step procedure.
explanations.notes_quote: Player 1 cuts the cake into two pieces ... Player 2 chooses the piece they prefer

explanations.text: The notes prove envy-freeness directly.
explanations.notes_quote: The Cut-and-Choose allocation is envy-free

explanations.text: For two players, envy-freeness implies proportionality, so the allocation is also proportional.
explanations.notes_quote: The Cut-and-Choose allocation is envy-free (and hence also proportional for $n = 2$)

explanations.text: The notes explicitly give examples showing it need not be equitable.
explanations.notes_quote: Not necessarily equitable

explanations.text: The complexity is stated explicitly in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Complexity: 2 operations under the Robertson-Webb model (1 Cut query + 1 Eval query)

questions.text: Which of the following statements about the Dubins-Spanier moving-knife algorithm are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A player shouts "Stop!" when the left segment is worth at least $1/n$ to them
The algorithm guarantees proportionality
For $n>2$, the algorithm is always envy-free
Its Robertson-Webb complexity is $\Theta(n^2)$
The last remaining player gets the remainder and is guaranteed value at least $1/n$ by the proof in the notes

explanations.text: This is the stopping rule in the procedure.
explanations.notes_quote: When any player finds the segment to the left of the knife worth at least $1/n$, they shout "Stop!"

explanations.text: The theorem in the notes proves proportionality.
explanations.notes_quote: The Dubins-Spanier allocation is proportional

explanations.text: The notes give a counterexample showing it is not envy-free for $n>2$.
explanations.notes_quote: Not envy-free for $n > 2$

explanations.text: The complexity theorem states this bound exactly.
explanations.notes_quote: the Dubins-Spanier algorithm requires $\Theta(n^2)$ operations

explanations.text: The proof argues the last player values each previous piece at at most $1/n$, so the remainder is worth at least $1/n$.
explanations.notes_quote: Hence the remaining piece is valued at at least $1 - (n-1)/n = 1/n$

questions.text: In the Dubins-Spanier worked example with three players, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In Round 1, Player 1 marks at $x_1 = 0.2$
In Round 1, the leftmost mark is Player 3 at approximately $0.667$
The final allocation is proportional but not envy-free
Player 2 envies Player 3 in the final allocation
Player 3 receives utility exactly $1/3$ in the final allocation

explanations.text: This is the first mark computed in the example.
explanations.notes_quote: P1: $(5/3)x = 1/3 \Rightarrow x = 0.2$

explanations.text: The leftmost mark is Player 1 at $0.2$, not Player 3.
explanations.notes_quote: Leftmost mark is P1 at $0.2$

explanations.text: This is the explicit conclusion of the example.
explanations.notes_quote: The allocation is proportional but NOT envy-free

explanations.text: The envy analysis states this directly.
explanations.notes_quote: P2 envies P3

explanations.text: Player 3 gets value $1$, not exactly $1/3$.
explanations.notes_quote: P3 gets $1$

questions.source_ref: Section 9: Algorithm 3: Even-Paz -- Optimal Proportionality for n Players
questions.text: Which of the following statements about the Even-Paz algorithm are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: It uses a divide-and-conquer approach based on median marks
For exposition, the notes describe it first for $n = 2^k$ players
Its proportionality proof is by induction over the recursion depth
Its Robertson-Webb complexity is $O(n^2)$, which is optimal for proportionality
The notes state a lower bound of $\Omega(n \log n)$ for any proportional cake-cutting algorithm, making Even-Paz optimal

explanations.text: The algorithm recursively splits players using the median cut.
explanations.notes_quote: Let $z^*$ be the $n/2$-th mark from the left (the median mark)

explanations.text: That is the simplifying presentation used in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: For ease of exposition, described for $n = 2^k$ players

explanations.text: The proof is explicitly by induction on the number of recursive halving steps.
explanations.notes_quote: Proof by induction

explanations.text: The complexity is $O(n \log n)$, not $O(n^2)$.
explanations.notes_quote: The Even-Paz algorithm achieves proportionality in $O(n \log n)$ operations

explanations.text: The lower bound and optimality claim are both stated explicitly.
explanations.notes_quote: any proportional cake-cutting algorithm requires $\Omega(n \log n)$ operations ... Hence, Even-Paz is optimal

questions.source_ref: Section 10: Algorithm 4: Selfridge-Conway -- Envy-Freeness for 3 Players
questions.text: Which of the following statements about the Selfridge-Conway algorithm are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: It guarantees envy-freeness for exactly three players
Player 1 begins by cutting the cake into three pieces they value equally
Player 2 trims the largest piece in their own view so that the two largest pieces become equal in their eyes
The algorithm guarantees connected pieces for all players
The notes state that proportionality follows because envy-freeness implies proportionality

explanations.text: This is the main purpose of the algorithm in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: The Selfridge-Conway algorithm guarantees envy-freeness for exactly three players

explanations.text: That is the first step of Stage 0.
explanations.notes_quote: Player 1 cuts the cake into three pieces they value equally

explanations.text: This trimming step is what creates the key structure for the later envy-freeness argument.
explanations.notes_quote: Player 2 trims the largest piece ... so that the two largest pieces are now equal in Player 2's eyes

explanations.text: The notes explicitly say connected pieces are not guaranteed.
explanations.notes_quote: Connected pieces | No (pieces may be disconnected)

explanations.text: The summary table states proportionality as a consequence of envy-freeness here.
explanations.notes_quote: Proportional | Yes (implied by EF)

sections.title: 7. Complexity of Envy-Free Algorithms and Strategyproofness

questions.source_ref: Section 11: Complexity of Envy-Free Algorithms (General n)
questions.text: Which of the following statements about envy-free cake-cutting complexity are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Brams and Taylor showed that an envy-free cake-cutting algorithm exists for any number of players under the Robertson-Webb model
The Brams-Taylor result gives a complexity bound depending only on $n$
Aziz and Mackenzie gave a bounded envy-free algorithm for any $n$, but with extremely high complexity
Procaccia proved a lower bound of $\Omega(n^2)$ operations for any envy-free cake-cutting algorithm
The notes emphasize a massive gap between the known upper and lower bounds for general envy-free cake cutting

explanations.text: This is the existence theorem stated in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: There exists an envy-free cake-cutting algorithm for any number of players under the Robertson-Webb model

explanations.text: The notes explicitly say the Brams-Taylor complexity cannot be bounded as a function of $n$ alone.
explanations.notes_quote: it cannot be bounded as a function of $n$ alone

explanations.text: This is the Aziz-Mackenzie bounded algorithm result.
explanations.notes_quote: A bounded envy-free algorithm exists for any $n$, but its complexity is extremely high

explanations.text: This is the lower bound theorem attributed to Procaccia.
explanations.notes_quote: Any envy-free cake-cutting algorithm requires $\Omega(n^2)$ operations

explanations.text: The notes explicitly highlight this unresolved gap.
explanations.notes_quote: There remains a massive gap between the upper bound ... and the lower bound $\Omega(n^2)$

questions.text: Which of the following statements about strategyproofness are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A cake-cutting algorithm is strategyproof if no agent can improve their allocation by answering queries non-truthfully
Cut-and-Choose is strategyproof because the cutter can always guarantee half the cake
The notes give an example where Agent 1 can benefit in Cut-and-Choose by cutting at $3/4-\varepsilon$ instead of $1/2$
There is no strategyproof algorithm for 2 agents that is both Pareto-optimal and envy-free
The notes state that Dubins-Spanier, Even-Paz, and Selfridge-Conway are also not strategyproof

explanations.text: This is the definition given in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: no agent can improve their allocation by answering the algorithm's queries non-truthfully

explanations.text: The notes explicitly show Cut-and-Choose is not strategyproof.
explanations.notes_quote: Cut-and-Choose is NOT Strategyproof

explanations.text: This is the concrete manipulation example used in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Agent 1 can cut the cake at $3/4 - \varepsilon$ instead of $1/2$

explanations.text: This impossibility theorem is stated explicitly.
explanations.notes_quote: There exists no strategyproof algorithm for 2 agents that is both Pareto-optimal and envy-free

explanations.text: The notes say the other algorithms discussed are also not strategyproof.
explanations.notes_quote: The other algorithms we have seen (Dubins-Spanier, Even-Paz, Selfridge-Conway) are also not strategyproof

sections.title: 8. Summary, Tutorial Problems, and Key Takeaways

questions.text: Which of the following entries correctly match the complexity summary table? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Cut-and-Choose: $n=2$, proportional plus envy-free, $O(1)$ (2 queries)
Dubins-Spanier: $n \geq 2$, envy-free, $O(n \log n)$
Even-Paz: $n \geq 2$, proportional, $O(n \log n)$, optimal for proportionality
Selfridge-Conway: $n=3$, envy-free, bounded constant complexity
Aziz-Mackenzie: any $n$, envy-free, with an astronomically large upper bound

explanations.text: This matches the first row of the summary table.
explanations.notes_quote: Cut-and-Choose | $n = 2$ | Proportional + Envy-free | $O(1)$ (2 queries)

explanations.text: Dubins-Spanier guarantees proportionality, not envy-freeness, and has $\Theta(n^2)$ complexity.
explanations.notes_quote: Dubins-Spanier | $n \geq 2$ | Proportional | $\Theta(n^2)$

explanations.text: This is exactly how Even-Paz is summarized.
explanations.notes_quote: Even-Paz | $n \geq 2$ | Proportional | $O(n \log n)$ | Yes (optimal for proportionality)

explanations.text: The table lists Selfridge-Conway as bounded and constant for three players.
explanations.notes_quote: Selfridge-Conway | $n = 3$ | Envy-free | $O(1)$ (bounded, constant)

explanations.text: This matches the final row of the table.
explanations.notes_quote: Aziz-Mackenzie | any $n$ | Envy-free | $O(n^{n^{n^{n^{n^n}}}})$

questions.source_ref: Section 14.1: Problem 1; Section 14.2: Problem 2; Section 14.3: Problem 3; Section 15: Key Takeaways
questions.text: Which of the following statements correctly reflect the tutorial problems and key takeaways? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In the moving-knife variant from Problem 1, the resulting allocation is shown to be $1/3$-envy-free
Problem 2 shows that for two agents, a connected allocation guaranteeing Alice at least $0.6$ and Bob at least $0.4$ always exists
Without the connectivity constraint in Problem 2, the notes show how to guarantee Alice at least $0.6$ and Bob at least $0.4$ by duplicating agents and using a proportional protocol
Problem 3 shows that requiring proportionality can cause a large social welfare loss, with the proportional solution achieving only $O(1/\sqrt{n})$ of the unconstrained optimum
One of the key takeaways is that no strategyproof algorithm exists that is simultaneously Pareto-optimal and envy-free, even for 2 agents

explanations.text: That is the exact claim proved in Problem 1.
explanations.section_ref: Section 14.1: Problem 1: $1/3$-Envy-Freeness of a Moving Knife Variant
explanations.notes_quote: The resulting allocation is $1/3$-envy-free

explanations.text: Problem 2 gives a counterexample showing this is not always possible with connectivity fixed in that way.
explanations.notes_quote: Answer: No. Counterexample

explanations.text: The notes use 3 copies of Alice and 2 copies of Bob, then apply a proportional protocol.
explanations.notes_quote: Create 3 copies of Alice and 2 copies of Bob (5 agents total), run any proportional protocol

explanations.text: This is the conclusion of the price-of-proportionality example.
explanations.notes_quote: The proportional allocation achieves only $O(1/\sqrt{n})$ of the socially optimal solution

explanations.text: This appears both in the strategyproofness section and in the key takeaways.
explanations.notes_quote: No strategyproof algorithm exists that is simultaneously Pareto-optimal and envy-free, even for 2 agents
